title: Add scope-boundary declaration to heavy-agent dispatch
description: Before dispatching to heavy-tier agents (writer, researcher, analyst), inject a mandatory scope-declaration step: the agent must explicitly state what it will and won't attempt before executing. This is a pre-flight capability signal, not a post-hoc error.
rationale: The reading note's core insight is that expectation-calibration should be designed into the system rather than delivered by failure. In Mira's current architecture, heavy agents (writer, researcher, analyst) accept tasks and proceed silently until they succeed or fail. There is no designed friction point where the system surfaces its actual scope confidence before execution. This mirrors the Claude Code failure pattern exactly: the system takes on tasks that feel within reach, runs until it hits a wall, and the user only learns the boundary through failure. The fix is to build the calibration signal into the dispatch layer itself.
diff_description: In task_worker.py, for agents with tier='heavy', prepend a scope-declaration phase before the main task prompt. The declaration prompt instructs the agent to output: (1) what it will attempt, (2) what it explicitly will not attempt in this run, and (3) any preconditions that must hold for the output to be reliable. This output goes to notes_outbox before the main execution begins — giving the user (or orchestrator) a chance to abort or narrow scope before compute is spent. In the writer prompt pipeline, add a 'scope' step as the first stage of plan/write/review/finalize: the writer agent must emit a one-paragraph scope statement before generating the plan. This is the designed friction: the agent announces its capability boundary rather than discovering it mid-execution.
